lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The break room is exactly as he left it. The hum. The fluorescent that pitches slightly sharp on cold nights. The stack.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Reza pulls the chart flat on the table and reaches for exam gloves from the dispenser by the sink. This is absurd. He pulls them on anyway.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Old habit: don't contaminate the data. He registers what that instinct means, and something tightens in his chest. He is treating this like a scene.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The entry sits midway through the chart, wedged between two nursing notes in handwriting he recognizes as his own. Four lines. Dates, dosage, attending signature. Routine language, clipped and precise.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: His handwriting loops at the descenders. Whoever wrote this does not.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He checks the date. February fourth. The night of the Okafor admission. The near-miss that started all of this.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He reads the entry again. A third time.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The note corrects a dosage discrepancy. It names the discrepancy explicitly: a decimal point entered one position to the right. A ten-fold error. The correction brought the patient back into range before harm occurred.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: If the note is accurate, the decimal point was not his mistake. If the note is accurate, someone else caught the error and charted the fix without attribution. If the note is accurate, he has spent four years carrying an event someone else created and someone else quietly resolved.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Reza sits with that.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The clock on the microwave reads 02:47. Through the small window over the sink, the parking lot runs empty under the sodium lights, the overnight staff cars lined up in a row.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He thinks about what it takes to correct a record and keep your own name off it. Access, authority, a reason to stay invisible. He thinks about who holds those things and who answers to them, and the answer is a very short list.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He thinks about Theo, which is unexpected, because Theo is not in this story yet. Except Reza has watched the way Harte moves around him — the administrative attention, the informal check-ins that are not check-ins. Reza has heard nothing specific and understood everything general: that there are people in this building who cannot afford to surface, who weigh every decision against a ledger that has nothing to do with medicine, and that the institution knows this and uses it. Power works that way. It finds the seam where someone is most exposed and leans.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He photographs the entry with his phone. Three angles. Sets the chart face-down.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Then he takes a clean sheet from the printer tray and copies the handwriting out slowly, tracing the loops, the sharp backward angle on the capitals. Cross-referencing against memory.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He knows this handwriting. Not from the chart — from the whiteboards in the east wing conference room. From printed agendas pinned to the departmental board with the notation Chaired: above the first item.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The attending who supervised his intern year. The man who signed his first probation report. The man who has held a board seat for sixteen years.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Reza folds the comparison sheet and puts it in his breast pocket. He seals off what he is feeling. Not yet. There is still most of a shift to run.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The chart goes back in the stack. The stack goes back where he found it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He strips the gloves and drops them in the bin. Stands at the sink a moment, not running the water, just standing. The break room hum fills the silence the way the break room always fills it — completely, indifferently, without rest. He is very still inside it. The door waits. He is not ready yet.
